variant_spelling: artificial; Old French artificial; Latin artificialis
short_summary: Gives artificial as late-14c. from Old French/Latin, earliest English phrase artificial day, made-by-human-skill sense from early 15c., imitation from 16c., insincere from 1590s, not-genuine from 1640s.
notes: Strong guide but exact dates need OED/primary confirmation.

short_summary: Gives Middle English artificial and Latin artificialis/artificium chain from ars skill plus facere make/do.
notes: Good independent confirmation that the word predates 1828.

short_summary: Gives artificially as an early-15c. adverb meaning by art, human skill, and contrivance.
notes: Important because the adverb preserves the art/skill/contrivance layer directly.

short_summary: Gives artifice first as workmanship/making by craft or skill, with crafty device/trick later.
notes: Strong bridge evidence; direct OED would improve confidence.

short_summary: Gives artificer as one who makes by art or skill, with later devious-artifice specialization.
Secondary/scholarly references report Johnson senses: made by art/not natural; fictitious/not genuine; artful/contrived with skill.
notes: Useful pointer but direct Johnson entry still needs manual verification.

short_summary: Secondary transcription gives artifice as trick/fraud/stratagem and art/trade.
notes: Confirms bridge shape if verified directly.
